content.text:
<plugin_info kind="matched_installed">
display_name: Figma
description: Plugin that includes the Figma MCP server and Skills for common workflows
skills:
  - figma-code-connect: Creates and maintains Figma Code Connect template files that map Figma components to code snippets. Use when the user mentions Code Connect, Figma component mapping, design-to-code translation, or asks to create/update .figma.ts or .figma.js files.
  - figma-create-new-file: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `create_new_file` tool call. NEVER call `create_new_file` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user wants a new blank Figma file — a new design, FigJam, or Slides file — or when you need a fresh file before calling `use_figma`. Usage — /figma-create-new-file [editorType] [fileName] (e.g. /figma-create-new-file figjam My Whiteboard, /figma-create-new-file slides Q3 Review)
  - figma-generate-design: Use this skill alongside figma-use when the task involves translating an application page, view, or multi-section layout into Figma. Triggers: 'write to Figma', 'create in Figma from code', 'push page to Figma', 'take this app/page and build it in Figma', 'create a screen', 'build a landing page in Figma', 'update the Figma screen to match code', 'convert this modal/dialog/drawer/panel to Figma'. This is the preferred workflow skill whenever the user wants to build or update a full page, modal, dialog, drawer, sidebar, panel, or any composed multi-section view in Figma from code or a description. Discovers design system components, variables, and styles from Code Connect files, existing screens, and library search, then imports them and assembles views incrementally section-by-section using design system tokens instead of hardcoded values.
  - figma-generate-diagram: MANDATORY prerequisite — load this skill BEFORE every `generate_diagram` tool call. NEVER call `generate_diagram` directly without loading this skill first. Trigger whenever the user asks to create, generate, draw, render, sketch, or build a diagram — flowchart, architecture diagram, sequence diagram, ERD or entity-relationship diagram, state diagram or state machine, gantt chart, or timeline. Also trigger when the user mentions Mermaid syntax or wants a system architecture, decision tree, dependency graph, API call flow, auth handshake, schema, or pipeline visualized in FigJam. Routes to type-specific guidance, sets universal Mermaid constraints, and tells you when to use a different diagram type or skip the tool entirely (mindmaps, pie charts, class diagrams, etc.).
  - figma-generate-library: Build or update a professional-grade design system in Figma from a codebase. Use when the user wants to create variables/tokens, build component libraries, set up theming (light/dark modes), document foundations, or reconcile gaps between code and Figma. This skill teaches WHAT to build and in WHAT ORDER — it complements the `figma-use` skill which teaches HOW to call the Plugin API. Both skills should be loaded together.
  - figma-use: **MANDATORY prerequisite** — you MUST invoke this skill BEFORE every `use_figma` tool call. NEVER call `use_figma` directly without loading this skill first. Skipping it causes common, hard-to-debug failures. Trigger whenever the user wants to perform a write action or a unique read action that requires JavaScript execution in the Figma file context — e.g. create/edit/delete nodes, set up variables or tokens, build components and variants, modify auto-layout or fills, bind variables to properties, or inspect file structure programmatically.
  - figma-use-figjam: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the FigJam context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
  - figma-use-slides: This skill helps agents use Figma's use_figma MCP tool in the Slides context. Can be used alongside figma-use which has foundational context for using the use_figma tool.
mcp_servers:
  - figma
</plugin_info>

content.text:
Here is what was implemented.

### What changed

Updates are all in `CategoryPill.tsx`, which already splits **Mountain / Accessory** (`frosted`) from **All / Electric / Road** (solid R166 inactive), matching your Figma refs ([1:61](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-61&m=dev) vs [1:53](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-53&m=dev)).

1. **Frosted pills (Mountain & Accessory)**  
   - Icons use **`rgba(255,255,255,0.48)`** so they read lighter and more “through” the glass.  
   - No extra shadow on the glyph (aligned with the flat mountain treatment in dev).

2. **Solid R166 pills (All, Electric, Road)**  
   - Icons use **`rgba(255,255,255,0.92)`** instead of `COLORS.textLo` so they read closer to the opaque Road icon in Figma.  
   - Icons sit in a small wrapper with **`shadowColor: #10141C`**, **`shadowOffset` ~2pt**, **`shadowOpacity` 0.55**, **`shadowRadius` ~3.5pt** (scaled with width), plus **`elevation: 6`** on Android.  
   - The **“All”** label gets the same higher-contrast color and a light **`textShadow`** so it matches the embossed feel of the icon row.

Active pills are unchanged (still full white on the accent gradient).

`npx tsc --noEmit` completes successfully. If the SVG shadow looks too soft on a device, we can nudge `iconShY` / `shadowOpacity` slightly; iOS draws this shadow from the icon’s alpha inside the clipped pill, so it should stay within the 50×50 chrome.

[REDACTED]

Here is what was updated in `CategoryPill.tsx`:

### Unavailable (Mountain & Accessory, `frosted`)
- Icons still use **white**, only **more transparent**: `rgba(255,255,255,0.28)` (was `0.48`), so they read as the same hue but clearly dimmed.
- **No** icon drop shadow (unchanged).

### Available (All, Electric, Road, solid R166)
- Icons and the **All** label use **`COLORS.white`** (fully opaque).
- **Stronger drop shadow**: a bit more offset (`3` vs `2` pt scaled), larger radius (`4` vs `3.5`), **`shadowOpacity` 0.65**, **`elevation` 8** on Android; label shadow matched a bit stronger.

The `frosted` prop is only documented to mean “unavailable” glass treatment; `app/index.tsx` wiring is unchanged.

If `0.28` feels too weak or too strong on your device, say what you prefer and we can nudge that single alpha.

content.text:
Here’s what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Cause
`TouchableOpacity` lowers the **opacity of its whole subtree** while you press (default `activeOpacity={0.85}`). That can make icons look **washed or “lighter”** during press, and on some builds/devices it can feel like the control **never quite returns** to the same look after you change selection—especially next to shadows and SVGs.

### Fixes (in `CategoryPill.tsx`)
1. **`Pressable` instead of `TouchableOpacity`** on all three variants (active, frosted, solid). Press feedback is **`android_ripple`** only on Android; **iOS no longer dims the pill or the icon** on tap, so inactive colors stay stable when you select/deselect.
2. **Stable text styles**  
   - Available inactive **“All”** uses a dedicated **`labelAvailable`** style with **`COLORS.white`**, not `labelInactive` + override (so we never mix in `textLo` by accident).  
   - Frosted text fallback uses **`labelUnavailable`** with the same **`ICON_UNAVAILABLE`** tint as the icons.
3. **Tints**  
   - Unavailable: **`rgba(255,255,255,0.42)`** — still white, clearly dimmer than available, less extreme than `0.28` on top of blur.  
   - Available: still **`COLORS.white`**.
4. **Glyph shadow** slightly toned down (`shadowOpacity` **0.5**, smaller radius) so it reads as depth, not a gray halo that fights the white icon.

`collapsable={false}` is set on the icon shadow wrapper for more reliable shadow layout on Android.

If ripple on the **active** gradient pill looks odd on a device, we can turn off `foreground: true` or narrow ripple to the solid pills only.

[REDACTED]

[REDACTED]

content.text:
Here is what was going wrong and what we changed.

### Causes
1. **`rgba(255,255,255,0.28)` on the SVG** – Premultiplied / platform-specific handling of alpha in `react-native-svg` can make fills look wrong or inconsistent vs. true `#FFFFFF`.
2. **`TouchableOpacity` (`activeOpacity={0.85}`)** – It lowers opacity on the **whole** subtree while pressed. That can make icons look “off” and, in some cases, feel like they stay wrong after you change selection.

### Fixes
1. **Mountain & Accessory** – Icons always use **`color={COLORS.white}`** (`#FFFFFF`). Transparency is **only** from a wrapper:  
   `opacity: UNAVAILABLE_ICON_OPACITY` (currently **`0.34`**), so the hue matches the other pills and they only read as “dimmed / unavailable.”
2. **All pills** – **`activeOpacity={1}`** on every `TouchableOpacity` so taps no longer fade the icon color.
3. **All / Electric / Road** – Still **`COLORS.white`** with the same drop shadow; labels use a dedicated **`labelAvailable`** style (full white) instead of mixing in `labelInactive` / `textLo`.

Tunable single constant at the top of `CategoryPill.tsx`:

```ts
const UNAVAILABLE_ICON_OPACITY = 0.34;
```

Lower it (e.g. `0.26`) for a more “ghosted” look, or raise it (e.g. `0.42`) if they should read a bit stronger on the glass.

[REDACTED]

Here is what was wrong and what we changed.

### What you wanted
For Mountain / Accessory, [Figma 1:61](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-61&m=dev) is a **transparent tile** (no filled squircle): the Discover background shows through, with a softer icon on top—not a darker icon from lowering opacity on top of a glass fill.

### What we did
For the `frosted` (unavailable) branch we **removed** the frosted stack entirely (`MaskedView`, `BlurView`, and the R167-style gradient overlays on native and web).

That branch is now:
- **`backgroundColor: 'transparent'`** on the same 50×50 hit area  
- **No** `glowWrap` / **no** `dropBelow` pill shadows (so you don’t get a “ghost” card behind an empty tile)  
- Icons use **`COLORS.textLo`** so they stay readable on `#242C3B` without a pill behind them (same token as other secondary labels)

Available pills (All / Electric / Road) are unchanged: R166 gradient, white icon, drop shadow.

Imports cleaned up: `@react-native-masked-view/masked-view`, `expo-blur`, and `Platform` are no longer used in this file.

If you want those icons closer to full white on the transparent tile, we can switch them to `COLORS.white` with a light `opacity` on the icon only, while keeping the background fully transparent.

[REDACTED]

content.text:
Here’s what changed for Mountain / Accessory (`frosted`):

### Semi-transparent pill
They no longer use a fully clear tile. They use the same **R166 gradient stops** as the solid pills (`#353F54` → `#222834`), but at **50% opacity** so the Discover background still shows through:

```ts
const UNAVAILABLE_GRADIENT = [
  'rgba(53, 63, 84, 0.5)',
  'rgba(34, 40, 52, 0.5)',
] as const;
```

Layout matches the solid branch: clipped rounded rect + `LinearGradient` + centered content (no heavy blur).

### Icons / label
Glyphs use **`rgba(255,255,255,0.72)`** so they stay readable on the tinted glass without matching the fully opaque “available” pills.

To make the tile **more** see-through, lower both alphas (e.g. `0.38`); for a **stronger** tint, raise them (e.g. `0.62`).

[REDACTED]

content.text:
Here is what was adjusted in `app/index.tsx` to pull the category strip closer to [Figma Discover 1:38](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-38&m=dev) (hero → categories → Items band).

### Spacing updates (`lx` tokens)

| Token | Before | After | Effect |
|--------|--------|-------|--------|
| `heroMb` | `20` pt | `10` pt | Less space **above** the row (below the hero card). |
| `catPv` | `4` pt | `2` pt | Less **internal** vertical padding on the horizontal category `ScrollView`. |
| `catScrollMb` | `12` pt | `4` pt | Less space **below** the row (above the product grid). |

`catMinH` is still `90` pt scaled so the diagonal stagger (`staggerStep` × 4 + 50 pt pill) is not clipped.

If it still feels loose or too tight on a device, the main levers are `heroMb` (hero ↔ pills) and `catScrollMb` (pills ↔ grid).

[REDACTED]

Tightening spacing: reducing hero gap, removing category strip padding, pulling the row up slightly, and slightly reducing the grid's left-column top offset to close the gap above the left column.

[REDACTED]

input.description: Typecheck after spacing and lx updates

content.text:
Here’s what changed in `app/index.tsx` to pull everything in tighter:

### Hero → categories
- **`heroMb`**: `10` → **`2`** pt (scaled), so the strip sits much closer under the hero card.
- **`catPullUp`**: new **`8`** pt (scaled) **negative `marginTop`** on the horizontal category `ScrollView`, so the row shifts up slightly (similar to how categories sit a bit under the hero in Figma).

### Category strip itself
- **`catPv`**: **`0`** (no extra vertical padding inside the row).
- **`catScrollMb`**: **`0`** (no margin under the row before the grid).

### Shorter diagonal (less vertical “air”)
- **`staggerStep`**: `10` → **`8`** pt — a bit less step between pills.
- **`catMinH`**: `90` → **`82`** (`50` pill + `4 × 8` stagger) so the strip isn’t taller than the stagger needs.

### Grid
- **`leftColumnTopOffset={22}`** on `DiscoverProductGrid` (default was `31`), so the left column starts a bit higher and the band under the categories feels tighter.

`tsc` passes. If the row feels too tight against the hero or overlaps “30% Off”, lower **`catPullUp`** (e.g. `4`) or raise **`heroMb`** slightly.

[REDACTED]

<user_query>
review the glassmorphic design on this Component in the figma mikkel-bech-yjAFnkLtKY0-unsplash-removebg-preview 5
</user_query>

Tracing the hero card implementation and fetching Figma context for the Top Card / glass treatment.

[REDACTED]

Here is a concise design review of the **Top Card / “mikkel-bech-yjAFnkLtKY0-unsplash-removebg-preview 5”** glass treatment versus what you have in code.

## What Figma shows ([Top Card `1:139`](https://www.figma.com/design/VYIhA7NSt2WzRJEzZNSeNY/Design-To-Ship--Copy---Copy-?node-id=1-38&m=dev))

In Dev Mode export, **`1:140`** is mainly a **large, cropped photo** (`mikkel-bech…`) filling the card frame (with big negative insets so it bleeds past the edges). **`1:141`** is the electric bike on top, **`1:142`–`1:143`** is “30% Off”. The file does not spell out separate “backdrop blur + tint” layers in that snippet; the frosted look may come from the asset, effects, or a flattened preview. Visually, the reference reads as: **soft frosted panel, cool dark fill, light top/left edge, soft drop shadow,** content sitting on top.

## What `HeroBackground` does (implementation)

```18:20:components/HeroBackground.tsx
 * Frosted / diffusion (Figma glass): iOS + Android use `MaskedView` + `expo-blur` `BlurView`
 * clipped to the hero path, then the R166 gradient tint at `FIGMA_HERO.overlayFillOpacity`.
 * Web uses a single semi-opaque vector fill (`fillOpacityFlat`) — no reliable masked blur.
```

- **Shape**: Custom SVG path with rounded top and slanted bottom — aligned with the hero chrome, not a generic rounded rect.
- **Native glass stack**: `MaskedView` → `BlurView` (dark tint) → R166 gradient fill at **`overlayFillOpacity: 0.52`** — sensible “frost + navy tint” stack.
- **Rim light**: Same idea as Figma’s upper highlight — **white → transparent** stroke gradient at **20% opacity, 2pt** (matches your `paint1_linear` comment).
- **Depth**: **Native shadow** on the wrapper (`#10141C`, offset ~20, radius ~18, opacity 0.55, elevation) instead of SVG `feDropShadow` — appropriate for RN.

```24:30:constants/theme.ts
export const FIGMA_HERO = {
  blurIntensity: 52,
  overlayFillOpacity: 0.52,
  fillOpacityFlat: 0.92,
} as const;
```

## Where it matches vs diverges

| Aspect | Match | Notes |
|--------|--------|--------|
| Frosted volume + cool tint | Strong | R166 gradient over blur reads like the reference’s dark blue-gray glass. |
| Edge highlight | Strong | Linear stroke approximates the light top/left rim. |
| Floating shadow | Strong | Soft dark lift under the card. |
| **Content behind glass** | Different | Figma’s **`1:140`** is a **photo fill** inside the card. In the app, blur sees **live UI behind the card** (accent polygon, `#242C3B`, scroll), and the **bike** is a separate `Image` in `index.tsx`, not the mikkel layer. So the “grain” and color behind the frost are not the same as the Figma composite unless you deliberately add a bitmap or static plate inside the mask. |
| **Web** | Weaker | `fillOpacityFlat: 0.92` is a **flat** imitation — no real backdrop blur, so glass reads more like a solid tinted panel. |

## Practical takeaways

1. **Native iOS/Android**: Architecturally you are doing real glassmorphism (mask + blur + tint + rim + shadow). Tuning **`blurIntensity`** vs **`overlayFillOpacity`** is the main dial if it feels too milky or too clear.
2. **Fidelity to the named Figma node**: To mirror **`mikkel-bech…`** literally, you’d add that image **inside** the masked hero (under the tint, above or below blur depending on desired effect), scaled like Figma’s inset — optional; current approach prioritizes **dynamic blur** over **fixed photo chrome**.
3. **Web**: Expect the largest visual gap; improving parity would mean something like `backdrop-filter` (where supported) behind the same clip, not only higher `fillOpacityFlat`.

I did not change any code; this is a review only. If you want next steps in the repo, say whether you care more about **matching the mikkel bitmap**, **stronger rim / inner glow**, or **web blur parity**, and we can implement in that direction.

content.text:
Here’s what was updated so the hero chrome tracks your Figma glass reference (semi-transparent panel, cyan/navy visible through it, frosted blur, thin rim, soft shadow).

### `constants/theme.ts` — `FIGMA_HERO`

| Token | Before | After | Purpose |
|--------|--------|-------|--------|
| `blurIntensity` | 52 | **72** | Stronger backdrop-style blur. |
| `overlayFillOpacity` | 0.52 | **0.34** | Lighter R166 tint so the **accent + `#242C3B`** read through the glass. |
| `fillOpacityFlat` | 0.92 | **0.44** | Web-only fill is less “solid panel,” closer to the same transparency idea. |
| *(new)* `borderStrokeWidth` | — | **1** | ~1px rim like the spec. |
| *(new)* `borderStrokeOpacity` | — | **0.38** | Soft highlight without a heavy outline. |

### `components/HeroBackground.tsx`

- Native **`BlurView`** tint **`dark` → `default`** so the blur isn’t extra-darkened on top of your tint (helps the blue diagonal read through, closer to the comp).
- Border stroke now uses **`FIGMA_HERO.borderStrokeWidth`** / **`borderStrokeOpacity`** on both the native overlay and the web SVG path.

Bike placement, promo copy (`rgba(255,255,255,0.6)`), and the slanted SVG path are unchanged — they already matched the Figma frame math.

`tsc --noEmit` passes. If a device still looks too milky or too clear, nudge **`overlayFillOpacity`** first (e.g. `0.30`–`0.38`), then **`blurIntensity`**.

input.description: Verify TypeScript after hero blur tint change

content.text:
### What went wrong

The hero read **flat gray** because the stack was **too desaturated**:

1. **`tint="default"`** on `BlurView` — Expo’s default blur is a **neutral, light-gray frosted plate**. On top of a dark UI it mutes cyan/navy and reads “gray card.”
2. **`overlayFillOpacity: 0.34`** — the R166 blue-gray tint was **too weak**, so the blurred layer dominated instead of your brand fill.
3. **`blurIntensity: 72`** — very strong blur **averages** background color toward **mid-gray**, which makes the problem worse when the tint is also neutral.

So the glass was still “transparent,” but the **dominant perceived color** became gray, not `#353F54` / accent.

### What we changed

**`constants/theme.ts` — `FIGMA_HERO`**

- `blurIntensity`: **72 → 58** (less mud, more color from behind).
- `overlayFillOpacity`: **0.34 → 0.44** (R166 tint carries more cool blue again).
- `fillOpacityFlat`: **0.44 → 0.58** (web hero less washed-out).

**`components/HeroBackground.tsx` — `BlurView`**

- **`tint="default"` removed.**
- **iOS:** `tint="systemThinMaterialDark"` — dark **material** blur that stays in the **cool / navy** family instead of neutral gray.
- **Android:** `tint="dark"` — stable with `dimezisBlurView`; material tints are less predictable there.

`tsc` still passes.

If it still feels slightly gray on a specific device, the next knob is **`overlayFillOpacity`** (e.g. `0.48`) before touching blur again.

[REDACTED]

input.description: Typecheck after FIGMA_HERO and HeroBackground edits

content.text:
Here’s what was changed.

### More blur
In `constants/theme.ts`, **`FIGMA_HERO.blurIntensity`** is **`52 → 62`** so the masked `BlurView` behind the hero chrome is a bit stronger on native.

### Clearer on the right
1. **`FIGMA_HERO.gradientEndStopOpacity: 0.7`** — the **right** R166 stop (`#222834`) is no longer full strength, so more of the blurred layer shows through on that side.
2. **`HeroFillDefs`** — the fill gradient is now **mostly horizontal** (`x1="24" y1="200"` → `x2="366" y2="200"` in design space) so “left vs right” matches your reference instead of the old almost-vertical line (`146,143` → `164,292`), which read as top-to-bottom.

Native overlay still uses **`overlayFillOpacity: 0.52`** on the tinted path; the new stop opacity stacks with that so the **right** edge reads a bit clearer.

Web still uses the same gradient inside the flat fill path (no real blur); it picks up the same L→R tint and softer right stop.

Tuning knobs: `blurIntensity` (~55–70), `gradientEndStopOpacity` (~0.65–0.85 for subtler vs stronger clear-right).

[REDACTED]

Reducing the hero tint opacities so more blur shows through.

[REDACTED]

Updated **`FIGMA_HERO`** in `constants/theme.ts` so the hero reads less heavy:

| Token | Was | Now |
|--------|-----|-----|
| `overlayFillOpacity` | 0.52 | **0.38** — weaker R166 tint over the blur (native). |
| `gradientEndStopOpacity` | 0.70 | **0.55** — right side of the fill a bit more open. |
| `fillOpacityFlat` | 0.92 | **0.78** — web fallback a bit lighter. |

If it still feels thick, lower **`overlayFillOpacity`** first (e.g. **0.32**); if only the right should change, tweak **`gradientEndStopOpacity`** alone.

[REDACTED]

input.description: Typecheck after FIGMA_HERO.tint and HeroBackground update

content.text:
Here’s what changed.

### `FIGMA_HERO.tint` (new) in `constants/theme.ts`
The hero no longer uses **`FIGMA_PRODUCT_CARD.r166`** for the frosted overlay. It uses a **lighter pair** in the same blue-gray family as the grid cards:

| | R166 (cards) | Hero tint |
|---|----------------|-----------|
| Light stop | `#353F54` | **`#3F4C62`** |
| Dark stop | `#222834` | **`#343B4D`** |

Product cards, tab bar, and `COLORS.sheet*` are unchanged (still R166).

### `HeroBackground.tsx`
`HeroFillDefs` now paints from **`FIGMA_HERO.tint.fill0` / `.fill1`**, with the same horizontal gradient and **`gradientEndStopOpacity`** as before.

So the hero should read **brighter and closer to the card chrome** without washing out the blur. If you want it even lighter, bump the tint hexes a bit (e.g. `#445266` / `#39465a`).

[REDACTED]
